The $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ -HF treatment for oxide removal will be used whenever it is suspected that oxide contamination has occurred.

Uranium will be recovered by volatilization with fluorine from flush and fuel salts before changing from $35\%$ enriched to highly enriched uranium and at the end of the program before discarding the salt to waste.
A decay time of 30 days for flush salt and at least 90 days for a fully irradiated fuel batch is desirable to reduce the amount of volatile fission products.
In the flush or coolant salts, the precipitated solid would be BeO, which has a solubility of approximately 275 ppm at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ (see Fig.

If the flush salt is contaminated with fuel salt up to approximately 0.01 mole of zirconium per kg of salt ( $\sim$ 1% fuel in flush salt), there would be insufficient zirconium present to exceed the solubility of $\mathrm{ZrO_2}$ at $1112^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and any precipitate would be BeO.
The effect of $10\%$ contamination of the flush salt with fuel salt is shown in Fig.
2.2 indicate extrapolation of data above 0.5 mole of $\mathrm{Zr}^{4+}$ per kg of salt.
Table 2.l shows the maximum amount of oxide that can be tolerated before zirconium and uranium oxides precipitate in flush salt containing small amounts of fuel salt.
The ratio of zirconium to uranium in the precipitate will be 5:l at $932^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ or 3.8:l at $1112^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
With pure fuel salt the ratio is 1.5:l, and more than 14,000 ppm of oxide will be required before uranium will precipitate.

At greater than 175 ppm of oxide, BeO will precipitate.
2.3) with a mixture of $\mathbf{H}_2$ and HF gas.
The $\mathbf{H}_2$ will prevent excessive corrosion of the INOR-8 structural material by maintaining a reducing condition in the salt.
Hydrogen fluoride will be obtained in 100-lb cylinders.
One cylinder will provide sufficient HF for 92 hr of processing at a flow rate of 9 liters/min ( $H_2:HF = 10:1$ ).
Since heating of the cylinders above $125^\circ F$ is not recommended, there is a pressure alarm on the exit gas set at 25 psig.
The HF gas will pass through an electric heater to raise the temperature above $160^\circ F$ and reduce the molecular weight to 20 for accurate flow metering.
The equilibrium amount of nickel in solution with a 10:1 ratio of $\mathbf{H}_2$ to HF is less than 1 ppm.
Any chromium in the salt from corrosion is expected to be in the nonvolatile +2 or +3 valence state.
The line will be heated to $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to prevent condensation of $\mathrm{H}_{2}\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{HF}$ .

The salt temperature will then be increased to $1300 \pm 20^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and the hydrogen flow will be continued to remove dissolved hydrogen fluoride and to reduce the amount of $\mathrm{FeF_2}$ in solution.
It should be possible to reduce the dissolved iron to the 200-ppm level obtained in the salt production operation.
When the salt samples show that further reduction of iron is not practical, the hydrogen flow will be stopped, and the salt will be sparged with nitrogen for 8 hr to purge the gas space of hydrogen.
The trap will be maintained at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to prevent adsorption of HF.

Since the vapor pressure of HF over NaF is 1 atm at $532^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the trap could be operated at a somewhat lower temperature, but $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ will be required to prevent $\mathrm{UF_6}$ adsorption during fluorination (see Sect.
2.2.3), and this temperature was selected for both operations.
The temperature can be varied between 0 and $40^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

Since the ratio of water to hydrogen fluoride in the off-gas stream leaving the fuel storage tank will be a function of the oxide content of the salt, $^{1}$ the HF utilization versus the oxide content can be calculated from this relationship, as shown in Fig.
The amount of condensed liquid for a cold trap temperature of $0^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and an $\mathsf{H}_2$ -to-HF ratio of 10:1 is shown in Fig.
2.5 do not include the untrapped water, which should amount to less than $2\%$ and can be ignored.
Each siphoning will remove approximately 55 cc.
The caustic scrubber will be charged with 1300 liters of 2 M KOH prepared by dilution of a $45\%$ KOH solution.
Three 115-gal batches of 2 M $(10\%)$ KOH will be prepared in a portable mix tank in the high-bay area and charged through a line provided with a manual valve and a check valve.
Dilution to 2 M will be required because of the possibility of gel formation in KF solutions of greater than 2 M.
At 9.1-liters/min HF flow, the KOH will have to be replaced every 4 days when the final concentration is $0.35\mathrm{M}$ .
It will, therefore, be necessary to jet the KOH solution to the liquid waste tank and replace it with fresh caustic when a 100-lb HF cylinder has been consumed.
A total gas flow of about 100 liters/min should provide good agitation.

The temperature of the melt will be maintained as low as practical (~20 to $30^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ above the liquidus of $813^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ ) to keep corrosion and fission-product volutilization to a minimum.
Boiling points of some volatile fluorides are listed in Table 2.2.
A minimum of 0.5 mole of fluorine per mole of uranium will be required to convert all the $\mathrm{UF}_4$ to $\mathrm{UF}_5$ .
At 100 liters/min of fluorine, $\mathrm{UF}_6$ evolution can begin about 2 hr after the start of fluorination.
Since the vapor space between the salt and the first absorber is $\sim 1300$ liters, another 15 min will probably be required before absorption begins.

added or the system has operated 4 hr at 100 liters/min.

However, until more is known about the sparging efficiency and the effect of the fuel storage tank geometry and salt composition, the fluorine disposal system should be put in operation 2 hr after the start of fluorine flow with sufficient $\mathrm{SO}_2$ to react with 50 liters of fluorine per minute.
Corrosion can consume as much as 9 liters of fluorine per minute (0.5 mil/hr).
With the $35\%$ -enriched uranium fuel salt, it will be necessary to replace the absorbers two times.
Before this can be done, the salt must be sparged with helium for 2 hr to remove dissolved $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_6$ and fluorine and to purge the gas space of $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_6$ .

At the end of the fluorination, when $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_6$ is no longer detected in the gas stream entering the absorbers, the fluorine flow will be stopped and the batch sparged with nitrogen for 2 hr.
It will be necessary to replace the fluorine trailer every 2 hr at a fluorine flow rate of 100 liters/min or every 4 hr with one-half flow.
A corrosion rate of 0.5 mil/hr was experienced in the Volatility Pilot Plant in a nickel vessel.

Fluorination of the high-uranium-content salt (fuel salt C) may take as long as 24 hr, but recovery of small amounts of uranium from the flush salt and uranium recovery from highly enriched fuel should require much less time.
A 24-hr fluorination would cause an average corrosion of approximately $3\%$ of the fuel storage tank wall at 0.5 mil/hr.

Corrosion of the fuel storage tank may be somewhat less because of the smaller surface-to-volume ratio, the use of dilute fluorine, and the lower fluorination temperature; also, corrosion tests indicate that INOR-8 may be more corrosion resistant than nickel.
The bed will again be maintained at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , that is, above the decomposition temperature of the $\mathrm{UF_6\cdot 2NaF}$ complex ( $702^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ at $1000\mathrm{mm}$ ).
Sodium fluoride at $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ will remove greater than $90\%$ of the niobium and ruthenium from the fluorinator off-gas stream, and these will be the principal activities that

Chromium is troublesome not only because of the gamma activity of the $^{51}\mathrm{Cr}$ formed by neutron activation but also because of the inactive chromium that will collect in valves and small lines and cause plugging and seat leakage.
All piping to and from the NaF bed must be heated to above $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to prevent $\mathrm{UF_6}$ condensation.
Table 2.3 shows the expected behavior of the volatile fluorides on NaF.
The decontaminated $\mathrm{UF_6}$ gas from the $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ NaF bed will flow to five NaF absorbers in series, which are located in a sealed cubicle in the high-bay area.

At $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ the vapor pressure of $\mathrm{UF_6}$ over the $\mathrm{UF_6\cdot 2NaF}$ complex will be 0.1 mm, and uranium losses will be significant (0.132 g of U per minute with a flow of 100 liters/min).
the external pores with $\mathsf{UF}_6\cdot 2\mathsf{NaF}$ .<sup>2</sup> To obtain maximum capacity the cooling air should therefore be turned on as soon as a temperature rise is indicated.

Also, with low fluorine concentration at elevated temperatures there could be a reduction of $\mathsf{UF}_6\cdot 2\mathsf{NaF}$ to $\mathsf{UF}_5\cdot 2\mathsf{NaF}$ , which would remain with the NaF when the $\mathsf{UF}_6$ was desorbed from the NaF.
The maximum radiation level at contact will be less than $100~\mathrm{mr/hr}$ .
Since an average efficiency of less than $25\%$ is expected during fluorination, there will be a large excess of fluorine.
Both the $\mathrm{SO}_2$ and the $\mathbf{F}_2$ will be preheated electrically to 300 to $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and then fed into a Monel reactor wrapped with steam coils.
The reaction is strongly exothermic and proceeds smoothly at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The cell air will then go to the main filters and be discharged from the 100-ft containment stack.
The caustic scrubber will be charged with 1300 liters of 2 M KOH.
This will be sufficient caustic for the complete neutralization of 50 liters of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ per minute for 8 hr with a final molarity of 0.33.

Since some of the $\mathrm{SO}_2$ will be consumed by unreacted fluorine and the $\mathrm{SO}_2\mathrm{F}_2$ is expected to hydrolyze slowly, changing the caustic solution every 8 hr should provide a considerable margin of safety against the solution becoming acidic.
The 8-hr caustic cycle will begin at the start of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ flow (2 hr after the start of $\mathrm{F}_2$ flow).

When processing has been stopped for replacement of the caustic (and for salt sampling and replacement of the fluorine trailer), the batch will first be sparged with helium for 1 hr to remove dissolved $\mathbf{U}\mathbf{F}_6$ and $\mathbf{F}_2$ and reduce corrosion during downtime.
The main portion of the fuel-handling and -processing system is in the fuel-processing cell, immediately north of the drain tank cell in Building 7503, as shown in Figs.
Figures 3.3 and 3.4 are photographs of the fuel-processing cell and the operating area, respectively.
Since corrosion is expected to be very low during $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ -HF treatment and only two or three fluorinations are planned, maintenance problems are not expected to be severe.
The recommended method is described in report ORNL-2550 (ref.

3) and consists of (1) barren salt flushes to displace as much as possible of the irradiated salt, (2) aqueous ammonium oxalate flushes to remove the salt film, (3) nitric acid-aluminum nitrate flushes to remove metallic scale from the surfaces, and (4) sodium hydroxide-hydrogen peroxide-sodium tartrate flushes for gas line decontamination.
More details on these decontamination procedures are given in Section 5.3.
Since the fission afterheat load of a fuel batch that has decayed four days after a full-power year of operation will be greater than 25 kw (see Fig.
3.6) and the maximum expected heat loss from the fuel storage tank is approximately 18 kw, some method of cooling was required.
If the minimum expected heat loss (5 kw) from the tank were realized, cooling would be required after one full-power year of operation

A preliminary study<sup>4</sup> indicated that cooling by natural air convection was feasible with only $2\mathrm{l} / 2$ hr decay.
A maximum heat load of $72\mathrm{kw}$ was assumed with an air temperature
rise from 150 to $660^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
However, the cooling requirement in the fuel storage tank will be much less than $72\mathrm{kw}$ for the following reasons:
A minimum decay time of four days will be used instead of 2 l/2 hr.
A heat loss of 5 to $18\mathrm{kw}$ is expected through the tank insulation and by radiation.
A thin stainless steel sheet is installed in this annular space about 2 ft from the top of the tank to restrict air flow up the tank wall when the dampers are closed.
With a 30-kw load and 500 ft³/min of air flow (the nominal cell exhaust rate), an air temperature rise of approximately 200°F has been calculated.
The sparge line is a 1-in. pipe that is closed at the bottom and has four 1/2-in. holes $90^{\circ}$ apart near the bottom.
The salt dip tube lies on the bottom of the tank at the center to minimize holdup (approximately $0.1\%$ of a batch).
Other instruments provided are 13 surface-mounted thermocouples and a pressure-recorder alarm.
The vent valve in the off-gas line opens if the tank pressure exceeds the design pressure of 50 psi.
The tank pressure will alarm above the normal pressure of 5 psi to indicate any plugging in the off-gas line, trap, valves, absorbers, or caustic scrubber.
3.7 and design data are given in Table 3.2.
The gas enters on the outside of an internal cylindrical baffle and leaves inside the baffle, and thus the gas path is almost 2 ft for a 12-in. depth of pellets.
Gas velocities are kept below 4 ft/min to prevent carryover of fines containing absorbed fission-product fluorides.

It is desired to keep the temperature below $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ to minimize corrosion.
The air in the absorber cubicle is maintained at a 2-in. $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ negative pressure with respect to the high-bay area by an exhauster located in the fuel-processing cell.
This is an exhauster with a capacity of 250 cfm at 10.5 in. $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ .
It is driven by a 3/4-hp 3450-rpm 440-v 3-phase motor.
The suction side is connected to the cubicle by a 4-in. steel pipe routed through the space west of the cell.
A 4-in. plug cock is provided in the suction line in the cubicle, with an access flange on the cubicle, to permit closing the valve with the gasketed top in place for leak checking.
The trap consists of two 4 3/8-in.-diam, 10 1/2-in.-long canisters in series, each with a charcoal depth of 3/4 in.
The canisters are installed in a flanged 6-in.
Each canister contains 1.5 lb of 6-14 mesh charcoal and is rated to process air at a maximum of 25 ft³/min.
Each canister has an exposed surface of 1 ft².
The pressure drop through one canister at 25 ft³/min is 0.15 in.
At this point the maximum concentration of hydrogen in the air is $0.65\%$ with a hydrogen flow rate of 90 liters/min and a cell air exhaust of 500 cfm.
This is well below the lower explosive limit of hydrogen in air of $4\%$ .
The fuel-processing cell ventilation air and the vessel off-gas will pass through a 2-in.-deep 24- by 24-in.
Fiberglas prefilter and a 11 1/2-in.-deep 24- by 24-in.
There are three 12-in.-diam butterfly valves for isolation and for bypassing of the filters for replacement.
This equipment is located in the spare cell with sufficient space allowed for the addition of 2 ft of shielding (for a total of $3\frac{1}{2}$ ft) between the filters and the fuel-processing cell.
The fluorine used will contain up to $5\%$ HF, which could cause plugging of the $\mathrm{UF}_6$ (by formation of NaF·2HF, NaF·3HF, etc.) if not removed.
3.12 and described in Table 3.7 is provided in the fluorine line at the gas supply station.

The inlet of the trap is maintained at $212^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ by steam to prevent plugging (by prevention of the formation of the higher hydrogen fluoride complexes) because of the high partial pressure of HF.

The exit of the trap is water cooled to about $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , which is below the operating temperature of the $\mathrm{UF}_6$ absorbers, and the trap should therefore remove any HF that could otherwise collect in the absorbers.
The reaction is exothermic and liberates 16.4 kcal per mole of HF absorbed.

With a fluorine flow of 100 liters/min, a processing time of 10 hr, and an HF content of $5\%$ , the trap has sufficient capacity for the fluorination of five batches if only $50\%$ of the NaF is complexed to NaF·HF.
Since the average HF content is less than $5\%$ , one loading should have
This requires the addition of approximately 1.2 Btu/liter of gas or approximately 20 Btu/min at the maximum HF flow rate anticipated (17 liters/min).

Another 1 Btu/min of sensible heat is required to heat the gas from $120^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the temperature of the gas at 25 psig leaving the cylinder, to $180^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , the temperature required to reach the monomolecular form.
This 21 Btu/min is equivalent to 370 w.
Two tubular heaters are used to provide a total of 1120 w.
The heater is strapped to the outside of a 2-in.
Monel pipe about 22 in. long packed with nickel wool for heat transfer.
The wool is confined at the ends by 6 mesh Monel cloth.
The salt sampler in the fuel-processing system is mounted on the roof plugs over the fuel-processing cell and is connected directly to the top of the fuel storage tank by a vertical 1 l/2-in. pipe.
It is designed for a maximum of 14 psig instead of 50 psig.
Area 1C is protected from overpressure by a pressure relief valve that vents to area 2B which is vented to the cell through the space around the 1 l/2-in. sampling line.
Electrical power for the fuel-processing system is supplied by a 750-kva 480/240-v transformer feeding a load center on the east side of the remote maintenance practice cell on the 840-ft level.
This 208-v supply feeds two power panels, CP-A and CP-B, and starters for the two motorized dampers for the fuel storage tank cooling air.
The starter and switch for the cubicle blower are located at the load center but have a separate supply since the blower requires 440 v.

Most of the equipment and pipe heaters are controlled by switches or powerstats located at two heater control panels, HCP-12 and HCP-13, at the south end of the heater control area on the 840-ft level east of the cell block.
The line and equipment heaters are listed in Tables 3.8 and 3.9.

The pipe line and equipment heater, blower motor, and motorized damper leads are routed from the heater control panels through a junction box on the west side of the decontamination cell on the 840-ft level.

The helium supply to the fuel-processing system is from the 40-psig helium header in the water room.
At the fuel-processing panelboard in the high-bay area, this supply is reduced at a 20-psig transfer header and a 13.5-psig purge and sparge header.

The purge and sparge header has sufficient pressure (13.5 psig) to permit sparging a salt batch at 100 liters/min but has insufficient pressure to force salt over the loop in line 110 (14.6 psig, min).
This header is provided with a pressure-relief valve set at 14.0 psig, which will reseal at 12.6 psig.

PdIA-ACIndicate lack of negative pressure in the absorber cubicle, possibly due to blower failure or excessive absorber cooling air
PIA-CSIndicate positive pressure in the scrubber vent, possibly due to plugging of the flame arrester or activated-charcoal traps
PdIA-FPCIndicate lack of negative pressure in the fuel-processing cell, possibly due to fan failure, filter plugging, or excessive air inleakage
PIA 530Indicate a failure in the helium supply system or PCV 530
PIA 604Indicate a failure in the helium supply system or PCV 604
PRA 608Indicate high pressure in the fuel storage tank vapor space; this could be caused by plugging in the off-gas line, NaF trap, valve HCV 692, absorber train, or scrubber inlet
PICA 690Indicate the gas pressure in the fluorine trailer; with a full trailer pressure of 55 psig, an alarm at 20 psig will indicate the consumption of about 12,000 standard liters of fluorine and that replacement of the trailer is required
PdIA 694Indicate that the H2-HF-F2 gas pressure has been reduced to within 1 psig of the pressure in the fuel storage tank vapor space and there is danger of a back up of salt into gas supply line 690
PIA 696Indicate that the HF gas pressure in the cylinder is reaching a dangerous level; this could be caused by failure of the temperature control valve regulating the steam to the hot-water drum around the cylinder
FIA 608Indicate a lack of purge flow to the fuel storage tank and the possibility of backup of gaseous activity to the instrument cubicle; this could be caused by lack of helium pressure or flowmeter plugging or incorrect setting
TA-HFHIndicate insufficient heating of the HF gas, which could result in incorrect flow metering
The maximum cooling loads are approximately 2500 Btu/hr in the cold trap and an approximately 1200-Btu/hr heat loss from the piping and equipment.
A 1 l/2-hp water-cooled refrigeration unit should provide sufficient capacity for brine temperatures as low as $-20^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Brine is circulated by a canned-motor pump through insulated 3/4-in.-OD copper tubing.
The refrigeration unit and circulating pump are located on the 840-ft level west of the cell.

A decay period of at least four days should be allowed before transfer of a fully irradiated fuel batch to the fuel storage tank to keep the xenon level at maximum ground concentration below $2.5\mathrm{mr/hr}$ .
Under normal atmospheric conditions, this concentration would occur 325 meters from the stack.
It would decrease rapidly with distance and decay time, as indicated in Table 4.1 and Fig.

The overall decontamination factors required to limit the release of iodine to $50\%$ of the ORNL weekly limit<sup>6</sup> of 1 curie are listed in Table 4.2, along with the decontamination factors for the other volatile activities as determined by the maximum permissible concentration in air.

Decay Period (days)Decontamination Factor
IodineTelluriumNiobiumRutheniumAntimony
47.0 × 105574831<1
103.1 × 105264729<1
201.0 × 105144620<1
304.0 × 104114518<1
902284319<1

Tolerances: iodine, 0.5 curie total release; other fission products, $10\%$ of 40-hr MPC in air; $\chi_{\max} = 3.7 \times 10^{-5}$ $\mu \mathrm{c} / \mathrm{cc}$ for a release of 1 curie/sec at 325 meters from stack.

As indicated in Table 4.1, the diffusion factor for a stack release under normal conditions is at a maximum at a distance of 325 meters from the stack, and the ground concentration is a factor of 20 less at the nearest point outside the restricted area, as shown in Fig.
The MSRE stack is 100 ft high and has a flow capability of 20,000 ft³/min.
An effective stack height of 163 ft, or 50 meters, was calculated:
h _ {v \max } = \frac {4 .
4 3 \frac {u}{V _ {s}}} \times \frac {Q V _ {s}}{u} = 6 3 f t,
V _ {S} = \text {s t a c k v e l o c i t y}, 4 7 f t / s e c,
Q = \text {s t a c k f l o w ,} 3 3 3 \mathrm {f t} ^ {3} / \sec .
Exposures from gaseous activity are to be limited to $10\%$ of the MPC for a 40-hr week.
The amounts of volatile activity to be expected in a fuel salt batch irradiated for one full-power year are listed in Table 4.3 for various decay times.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: 4.3.1.1 Activity Released When Not Processing.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: A fully irradiated fuel batch will be held in the fuel drain tank, with the off-gas passed to a charcoal bed, until the xenon emission cannot cause a radiation level in excess of $2.5 \, \text{mr/hr}$ at the point of maximum ground concentration.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: 4.1 it can be seen that an approximately four-day decay period is required before transfer to the fuel storage tank, which is not vented through a charcoal bed.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Since there is about $50 \, \text{ft}^3$ of gas space in the processing equipment and the purge rate will be low when not processing, there probably will be no xenon emission from the stack until processing actually begins.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Because of the very short half life of $^{85}\text{Br}$ , $^{85}\text{Kr}$ emission will be below the MPC $30 \, \text{min}$ after reactor shutdown.

text: Nickel, which is the most noble of the structural metals will be limited to less than 1 ppm as NiF $_2$ with a 10:1 H $_2$ -to-HF ratio.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: 4.3.1.3 Activity Released During Fluorination.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Based on Chemical Technology Division fluorination experience, decontamination factors of 1 for iodine (complete volatilization), 1 to 10 for tellurium, and 10 to $10^{3}$ for niobium, ruthenium, and antimony can be expected.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: From Table 4.2 it can be seen that the only fission product that requires much additional decontamination is iodine, and this should be obtained in the activated-charcoal trap.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Additional decontamination from most fission products will be obtained by plateout in both the reactor and the fuel-processing system by the $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ NaF trap, by the caustic scrubber, and by the filters.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Total release of the 114 curies of iodine present in the fuel after 90 days of decay, while considerably in excess of the 1 curie/week maximum release as determined by the grass-cow-milk-human chain, would result in an exposure of only 0.3 rem in 8 hr at the point of maximum ground concentration.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: This is only $3\%$ of the permissible exposure due to accidental or unusual releases.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: The simultaneous rupture of both the fuel storage tank and the caustic scrubber and contact of the two volumes could cause a rapid buildup of the cell pressure to approximately 100 psig by vaporizing the 1600 liters of caustic solution and releasing the associated activity to the building and up the stack.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Contact is prevented by an 18-in.-high dam that divides the cell.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: If there should be a rupture in the gas space of the fuel storage tank or in the off-gas piping or equipment upstream of the caustic scrubber, the most serious release would occur during fluorination when fission-product volatilization is expected to be greater and processing times shorter than during the $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ -HF treatment.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: If the activity is evolved uniformly over a 10-hr period, $2\%$ of the activity will be in the gas space at any one time.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: If shutdown requires 6 min, another $1\%$ would be evolved for a total of $3\%$ .

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Assuming 90 days decay and complete volatilization of tellurium, the exposure at the point of maximum ground concentration would be less than $3\%$ of the permissible quarterly exposure.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: The leak would be detected by both cell air monitors and fluoride ion monitors, and it seems reasonable that fluorine flow could be stopped within 6 min.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Although corrosion rates as high as 0.5 mil/hr during fluorination are expected, the fluorination time will be short and processing will be infrequent, so the total corrosion will be small.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: The NaF trap and the absorbers are critically safe without internal moderation because of the maximum capacity of $0.9\mathrm{g}$ of uranium per gram of NaF.

section: 4.3.1 Activity Release from the Containment Stack
text: Since absorption will be very rapid and complete, there will be no possibility of breakthrough of more than 1 or $2\mathrm{g}$ of uranium.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: A negative pressure of 1 in. of water will be maintained during processing.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: Air inleakage should be less than 500 ft³/min by comparison with a Volatility Pilot Plant cell 50% larger and having considerably more penetrations and maintaining a 2-in. negative

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: 4.3.2.2 Activity Released from the Absorber Cubicle.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: The absorber cubicle is a sealed box of 3/16-in.-thick steel located near the instrument panel board in the high-bay area.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: The maximum cubicle pressure with $100\text{-ft}^3/\text{min}$ air flow will be checked to demonstrate that a pressure greater than 1 psig cannot be obtained with the blower off.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: If necessary the maximum air flow will be reduced below $100\text{ft}^3/\text{min}$ to prevent exceeding 1 psig.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: Prior to fluorination the vent valve will be closed, the cubicle pressure will be raised to 1 psig, and the leak rate will be determined.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: Leakage must be less than $1\%/\text{hr}$ (220 cc/min).

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: This rate of tellurium leakage would allow 14 min of working time in the high-bay area without masks.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: The fuel-processing system sampler, as mentioned in Section 3.4.6, is similar to the fuel-pump sampler-enricher.

section: 4.3.2 Activity Release to the Operating Area
text: 3.13) to a secondary containment area 3A, where the sample will be sealed inside a transport container tube before being removed from the sampler to a shielded carrier.

The radiation level in the high-bay area over the fuel-processing cell will be less than $10\mathrm{mr/hr}$ through the 4-ft-thick high-density-concrete roof plugs under the most severe conditions with a fully irradiated, four-day-decayed fuel salt batch in the fuel storage tank.
Operations will be planned to limit exposure of individuals to less than $100\mathrm{mr/week}$ , and signs will be posted indicating radiation levels at various points in the high-bay area.
The salt sampler will be shielded with 4 in. of lead, which should reduce the radiation level from a fuel salt sample to less than $10 \, \text{mr/hr}$ .
As mentioned in Section 2.2.4, the maximum radiation level expected at the surface of each absorber is less than $100 \, \text{mr/hr}$ .
The 4-ft area between the switch house and the cell will be filled with stacked concrete blocks.
When a fully irradiated fuel batch is in the storage tank, the level at the east wall of the switch house should be less than $5\mathrm{mr/hr}$ .
This space will provide for sufficient shielding (a total of 3 l/2 ft) to permit replacing the off-gas filters without exceeding planned exposures.
The dose rate should be less than 50 mr/hr.
4.4.1.4 Decontamination Cell.
The 18-in. north wall of the fuel-processing cell borders the decontamination cell.
4.4.1.5 Area Surrounding the Waste Cell.
The maximum activity expected in the caustic solution from the fluorination of a 90-day-decayed fuel batch is 1300 curies of tellurium, assuming $10\%$ removal in the scrubber.

In Volatility Pilot Plant run R-7, only $8\%$ of the tellurium was found in the scrubber, which is a much more efficient unit than the MSRE unit, which is designed primarily for the neutralization of HF.

If all the tellurium in a fully irradiated fuel salt batch allowed to decay for 90 days were evolved during the first 8 hr of fluorination (first batch of caustic solution), and $10\%$ were removed in the scrubber, 1300 curies would be collected in about 350 gal of liquid.
This is 3.7 curies/gal and could be sent to the intermediate level waste tanks at the Central Collection Station without dilution after being jetted to the MSRE liquid waste tank.
Some solution might back up the jet steam line, but this line is shielded to approximately 25 ft from the jet and backup this far into a closed line is unlikely with an open line to the waste tank.
Considerable time would be required to build up sufficient pressure (7 psig) to force solution up the sparge line to the absorber cubicle.
4.4.2.3 From Radioactive Gas.
Steps will be taken as indicated in Section 4.3.2.4 to keep gaseous activity out of the sampler.
Sparging for 1 hr with helium should reduce the activity in the gas space by a factor of $10^{5}$ .
Complete filling of the sampler access chamber with atmosphere from the fuel storage tank would result in a radiation level $10^{3}$ times less than that from a fully irradiated fuel salt sample.
Close V 994 and check buffer gas pressure.
Check leak detector flanges on HCV 692, HCV 694, and NaF trap.
Pressure should remain constant for 1 hr.
Check end of line 692 in absorber cubicle for leaks. b.
Pressurize sampler-valve (V 994) buffer gas supply and note effect on system pressure.

section: 5.1.2.1 Prepare and Test Piping in Cubicle
text: Install blind flange above V 693.
Close V 978 and install pipe cap over valve stem.
Open V 970 and V 970B until pressure in cubicle reaches 27 in. $\mathbf{H}_2\mathbf{O}$
Adjust V 970B to 220 cc/min.
If this is the case, close V 970 and stop the test when the cubicle pressure reaches 30 in. $\mathbf{H}_2\mathbf{O}$
If the pressure remains steady, check that the pressure does not fall in $4\mathrm{hr}$ .
Open V 970B until pressure in cubicle reaches 27 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$
Adjust V 970B to 150 cc/min.
If this is the case, close V 970B and stop the test when the cubicle pressure reaches 30 in. $\mathsf{H}_2\mathsf{O}$
If the pressure remains steady, check that the pressure does not fall in $4\mathrm{hr}$ .
Add 25.3 gal of $45\%$ KOH from a 55-gal drum to the makeup tank.
Open V 313 and tank drain valve.
Close valves and repeat steps 1 to 3 two times for a total addition of 343 gal of caustic.
Open V 607A and set flow at 100 liters/min.
When system has been purged for 2 hr, close V 607A.
Open V 607A and purge at 1 liter/min.
Open V 608A and purge at 5 liters/min.
Adjust temperature of NaF trap to $750 \pm 20^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Open steam valve to water bath and adjust temperature controller to $100^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
HF cylinder must not be heated above $122^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , which is equivalent to the PIA set point of 25 psig.
Set FIC 696 at 9 liters/min.
Check that HF temperature at flowmeter is $180^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Water bath temperature controller can be raised to a maximum of $122^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ if necessary to obtain the desired flow rate.
When molarity of caustic calculated from HF consumption falls to 0.35, stop $\mathbf{H}_2$ and HF flows, jet caustic to waste, charge fresh caustic, and resume treatment.
When the time between siphons is greater than 1 hr (oxide removal rate less than $2\mathrm{ppm / hr}$ ), heat the upper half and top of the FST to $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Sample salt every 8 hr until sample analyzes less than 10 ppm iron.
Open V 698D and increase nitrogen flow to 50 liters/min.
After 8 hr, stop the nitrogen flow.
Post radiation level signs if level exceeds 3 mrem/hr.
Start waste pump and circulate waste solution for 1 hr.
If level does not exceed 100 mrem/hr take two 5-ml samples by carefully opening V 305B.
5.1.5.3 Liquid Waste Dilution.
If activity level is greater than 10 curies/gal, water must be added to the liquid waste tank to reduce the activity to this level.
If the activity level is too great to permit sampling, dilute to 4000 gal.
This should be sufficient to bring the activity level well below 10 curies/gal for the expected activities from a fully irradiated fuel batch decayed for 90 days.
Close V 994 and check buffer gas supply.
Check leak detector flanges on HCV 692, HCV 694, and NaF trap and tighten if required.
Pressure should remain constant for 1 hr.
Check end of line 692 in absorber cubicle.
Pretreat 70 to $120\mathrm{kg}$ of NaF pellets by heating to $750^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for 1 hr with $\mathbf{N}_2$ purge.
Load each absorber with 14 to $24\mathrm{kg}$ of NaF pellets, depending on type salt being processed.
Pressure should remain constant for 1 hr.
Close V 978 and install pipe cap over valve stem.
Open V 970 and V 97OB until pressure in cubicle reaches 27 in. $\mathbf{H}_2\mathbf{O}$
Adjust V 970B to 200 cc/min.
If this is the case, close V 970 and stop the test when the cubicle pressure reaches 30 in. $\mathbf{H}_2\mathbf{O}$
If the pressure remains steady, check that pressure does not fall in $4\mathrm{hr}$ .
Add 25.3 gal of $45\%$ KOH from a 55-gal drum to the makeup tank.
Open V 313 and tank drain valve.
Close valves and repeat steps 1 to 3 two times for a total of 343 gal of caustic.
Open V 607A and set flow at 100 liters/min.
When system has been purged for 2 hr, close V 607A.
Open V 607A and set flow at 1 liter/min.
Open V 608A and set flow at 5 liters/min.
Adjust temperature of NaF trap to $750 \pm 20^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
If radioactive salt is to be treated with $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ -HF before fluorination, the system should be conditioned prior to that treatment.
Adjust temperature of NaF trap to 200 to $250^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Open V 698A and set PCV 698 to 20 psig.
Open V 698C and adjust V 698B to flow rate of 6.6 liters/min (5% excess).
Adjust V 607B for helium flow rate of 13.75 liters/min.
V 608B is still adjusted for 5 liters/min.
Adjust PCV 690 for fluorine flow of 6.25 liters/min.
Continue flow for 8 hr, and sample caustic solution (see Sect.
Analyze for $\mathbf{F}^{-}$ , $\mathbf{SO}_4^{2-}$ , and KOH molarity.
Adjust V 607B for helium flow rate of 7.5 liters/min.
Adjust V 698B for $\mathbf{SO}_2$ flow rate of 13.2 liters/min.
Continue flow for 8 hr, and sample caustic solution.
Adjust V 607B for helium flow rate of 1.25 liters/min.
Adjust V 698B for $\mathbf{SO}_2$ flow rate of 19.7 liters/min.
Continue flow for 8 hr and sample caustic solution.
Adjust V 698B for $\mathbf{SO}_2$ flow rate of 52.25 liters/min.
Continue flow for 4 hr and sample caustic solution.
Adjust V 698B for $\mathbf{SO}_2$ flow rate of 100 liters/min.
Continue flow for 4 hr, close V 690A, and sample caustic solution.
Adjust V 607B for helium flow rate of 50 liters/min.
Adjust V 608B for helium flow rate of 5 liters/min.
Continue $\mathbf{SO}_2$ flow for 1 hr after increasing helium flows.
Open V 970 to cool first absorber.
Reduce fluorine flow if temperature reaches $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Adjust PCV 698 for flow of 50 liters/min.
Check $\mathbf{SO}_2$ and $\mathbf{F}_2$ preheater temperatures and maintain at $400^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .
Reduce fluorine flow rate to 50 liters/min.
Start helium flow to FST by opening V 607A and adjusting V 607B to 50 liters/min.
Reduce pre-heater temperatures slightly if necessary to keep reactor temperature below $1000^{\circ}\mathbf{F}$ .
Adjust V 607B to 100 liters/min helium sparge for 1 hr.
Close V 970, 971, 972, 973, and 974 to stop air flow to the absorber cubicle.
After 15 min, check radiation monitors to see that there is no leak into the absorber cubicle.
If activity is present, open V 970 and purge cubicle until there is no air activity in the cubicle.
Since a fluorine trailer will only last 2 hr at 100 liters/min, it will be necessary to stop fluorination before all the $\mathrm{UF_6}$ is volutilized.
The salt will be sampled every 2 hr while the trailer is being changed to check on the progress of fluorination.
At alternate trailer changes (every 4 hr), it will be necessary to jet the caustic solution to the waste tank and charge fresh caustic.
Adjust V 607B to 100 liters/min helium sparge for 1 hr.
When uranium concentration is less than 25 ppm, heat the top half and top of the FST to $900^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and fluorinate for an additional 30 min.
Stop fluorine flow and repeat helium sparge for 1 hr and resample.
If sample shows less than 15 ppm uranium, the fluorination is complete.
Open V 312 and jet caustic to waste tank.
Disconnect fluorine trailer and return to K-25.
Start waste pump and circulate waste for 1 hr.
If level is not too high, take two 5-ml samples by carefully opening V 305B.
5.2.8.3 Liquid Waste Dilution.
If activity level is greater than 10 curies/gal, add water to the liquid waste tank to reduce the activity to this level.
If the activity level is too great to permit sampling, dilute to 4000 gal.
This should be sufficient to bring the activity level well below 10 curies/gal for the expected activities from a fully irradiated fuel batch decayed for 90 days.

As mentioned in Section 3.2, the MSRE fuel-processing system is designed for direct maintenance after decontamination, except for a few remotely maintainable items, such as the two air-operated valves and the NaF trap.
Sparge FST with $\mathbf{N}_2$ at 100 liters/min for 4 hr.
Continue sparging for 4 hr more and sample.
Connect charging line to flange upstream of V 693 in absorber cubicle and charge two batches to the caustic scrubber at room temperature.
Sparge FST with $\mathbf{N}_2$ at 100 liters/min for 1 hr.

82, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, November 1963.

244, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, January 1965.
